title: Reinforcement learning for instance segmentation with high-level priors

paragraphs.text:
## **Stateless actor-critic for instance segmentation with high-level priors**

## **Anonymous Author(s)**

Affiliation Address `email`

## **Abstract**

1 Instance segmentation is an important computer vision problem which remains 2 challenging despite impressive recent advances due to deep learning-based meth3 ods. Given sufficient training data, fully supervised methods can yield excellent 4 performance, but annotation of ground-truth data remains a major bottleneck, espe5 cially for biomedical applications where it has to be performed by domain experts. 6 The amount of labels required can be drastically reduced by using rules derived 7 from prior knowledge to guide the segmentation. However, these rules are in 8 general not differentiable and thus cannot be used with existing methods. Here, 9 we relax this requirement by using stateless actor critic reinforcement learning, 10 which enables non-differentiable rewards. We formulate the instance segmentation 11 problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven 12 by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to 13 high-level priors on object shape, position or size. The experiments on toy and real 14 datasets demonstrate that we can achieve excellent performance without any direct 15 supervision based only on a rich set of priors.

## 16 **1 Introduction**

- 17 Instance segmentation is the task of segmenting all objects in an image and assigning each of them 18 a different label. It forms the necessary first step to the analysis of individual objects in a scene 19 and is thus of paramount importance in many practical applications of computer vision. Over the 20 recent years, fully supervised instance segmentation methods have made tremendous progress both 21 in natural image applications and in scientific imaging, achieving excellent segmentations for very 22 difficult tasks [1, 2].

23 A large corpus of training images is hard to avoid when the segmentation method needs to take 24 into account the full variability of the natural world. However, in many practical segmentation 25 tasks the appearance of the objects can be expected to conform to certain rules which are known _a_ 26 _priori_ . Examples include surveillance, industrial quality control and especially medical and biological 27 imaging applications where full exploitation of such prior knowledge is particularly important as the 28 training data is sparse and difficult to acquire: pixelwise annotation of the necessary instance-level 29 groundtruth for a microscopy experiment can take weeks or even months of expert time. The use of 30 shape priors has a strong history in this domain [3, 4], but the most powerful learned shape models 31 still require groundtruth [5] and generic shapes are hard to combine with the CNN losses and other, 32 non-shape, priors. For many high-level priors it has already been demonstrated that integration of 33 the prior directly into the CNN loss can lead to superior segmentations while significantly reducing 34 the necessary amounts of training data [6]. However, the requirement of formulating the prior as 35 a differentiable function poses a severe limitation on the kinds of high-level knowledge that can 36 be exploited with such an approach. The aim of our contribution is to address this limitation and
Submitted to 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). Do not distribute.
- 37 establish a framework in which a rich set of non-differentiable rules and expectations can be used to 38 steer the network training.

- 39 To circumvent the requirement of a differentiable loss function, we turn to the reinforcement learning 40 paradigm, where the rewards can be computed from a non-differentiable cost function. We base 41 our framework on a stateless actor-critic setup [7], providing one of the first practical applications 42 of this important theoretical construct. In more detail, we solve the instance segmentation problem 43 as agglomeration of image superpixels, with the agent predicting the weights of the edges in the 44 superpixel region adjacency graph. Based on the predicted weights, the segmentation is obtained 45 through (non-differentiable) graph partitioning and the segmented objects are then evaluated by the 46 critic, which learns to approximate the rewards based on the object- and image-level reasoning (see 47 Fig. 1).

- 48 The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows: (i) we formulate instance segmen49 tation as a RL problem based on a stateless actor-critic setup, encapsulating the non-differentiable step 50 of instance extraction into the environment and thus achieving end-to-end learning; (ii) we exploit 51 prior knowledge on instance appearance and morphology by tying the rewards to the conformity of 52 the predicted objects to pre-defined rules and learning to approximate the (non-differentiable) reward 53 function with the critic; (iii) we introduce a strategy for spatial decomposition of rewards based on 54 fixed-sized subgraphs to enable localized supervision from combinations of object- and image-level 55 rules. (iv) we demonstrate the feasibility of our approach on synthetic and real images and show 56 an application to an important segmentation task in developmental biology, where our framework 57 delivers an excellent segmentation with no supervision other than high-level rules.

## 58 **2 Related work**

- 59 Reinforcement learning has so far not found significant adoption in the segmentation domain. The 60 closest to our work are two methods in which RL has been introduced to learn a sequence of 61 segmentation decision steps as a Markov Decision Process. In the actor critic framework of [8], the 62 actor recurrently predicts one instance mask at a time based on the gradient provided by the critic. 63 The training needs fully segmented images as supervision and the overall system, including an LSTM 64 sub-network between the encoder and the decoder, is fairly complex. In [9], the individual decision 65 steps correspond to merges of clusters while their sequence defines a hierarchical agglomeration 66 process on a superpixel graph. The reward function is based on Rand index and thus not differentiable, 67 but the overall framework requires full (super)pixelwise supervision for training.

- 68 Reward decomposition was introduced for multi agent RL by [10] where a global reward is decom69 posed into a per agent reward. [11] proves that a stateless RL setup with decomposed rewards requires 70 far less training samples than a RL setup with a global reward. In [12] reward decomposition is 71 applied both temporally and spatially for zero-shot inference on unseen environments by training on 72 locally selected samples to learn the underlying physics of the environment.

- 73 The restriction to differentiable losses is present in all application domains of deep learning. Common 74 ways to address it are usually based on a soft relaxation of the loss that can be differentiated. The 75 relaxation can be designed specifically for the loss, such as, for example, Area-under-Curve [13] for 76 classification or Jaccard Index [14] for semantic segmentation. These approaches are not directly 77 applicable to our use case as we aim to enable the use of a variety of object- and image-level priors 78 which can easily be combined without handcrafting an approximate loss for each case. More generally, 79 but still for a concrete task loss, Direct Loss Minimization has been proposed for CNN training in 80 [15]. For semi-supervised learning of a classification or ranking task, Discriminative Adversarial 81 Networks have been proposed as a means to learn an approximation to the loss [16]. Most generally, 82 Grabocka et al. in [17] propose to train a surrogate neural network which will serve as a smooth 83 approximation of the true loss. In our setup, the critic can informally be viewed as a surrogate network 84 as it learns to approximate the priors through the rewards by Q-learning.

- 85 Incorporation of rules and priors is particularly important in biomedical imaging applications, where 86 such knowledge can be exploited to augment or even substitute scarce groundtruth annotations. 87 For example, the shape prior is explicitly encoded in the popular nuclear [18] and cellular [19] 88 segmentation algorithms based on spatial embedding learning. Learned non-linear representations 89 of the shape are used in [5], while in [20] the loss for object boundary prediction is made topology90 aware. Domain-specific priors can also be exploited in post-processing by graph partitioning [21]. 91 Interestingly, the energy minimization procedure underlying the graph partitioning can also be 92 incorporated into the learning step [22, 23].

The rewards are given back to the agent where they are used for training.

## 93 **3 Methods**

- 94 The task of instance segmentation can be formalized as transforming an image _x_ into a labeling _y_ , 95 where _y_ maps each pixel to a label value. An instance corresponds to the maximal set of pixels with 96 the same label value. Typically, the instance segmentation problem is solved via supervised learning, 97 i.e. using a training set with ground-truth labels _y_ ˆ. Note that _y_ is invariant under the permutation 98 of label values. In general, it is difficult to formulate instance segmentation in a fully differentiable 99 manner. Most approaches first predict a "soft" representation with a CNN, e.g. affinities [1, 24, 25],

- 100 boundaries [26, 27] or embeddings [28, 29] and apply non-differentiable post-processing, such as 101 agglomeration [27, 30], clustering [31, 32] or partitioning [33], to obtain the instance segmentation. 102 Alternatively, proposal-based methods predict a bounding-box per instance and then predict the 103 instance mask for each bounding-box [34]. Furthermore, the common evaluation metrics for instance 104 segmentation [35, 36] are also not differentiable.

- 105 Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction 106 to differentiable losses and - regardless of the loss - to make the whole pipeline differentiable end-to107 end even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into 108 individual instances.

- 109 We formulate the instance segmentation problem using a region adjacency graph _G_ = ( _V, E_ ), 110 where the nodes _V_ correspond to superpixels (homogeneous clusters of pixels) and the edges _E_ 111 connect nodes which belong to spatially adjacent superpixels. Given edge weights _W_ , an instance 112 segmentation can be obtained by partitioning the graph, here using an approximate multicut solver 113 [37]. Together, the image data, superpixels, graph and the graph partitioning make up the environment 114 _E_ of our RL setup. Based on the state _s_ of _E_ , the agent _A_ predicts actions _a_ , which are used to 115 compute the partitioning. The reward _r_ is then computed based on this partitioning. Our agent _A_ is a 116 stateless actor-critic [38], represented by two graph neural networks (GNN) [39]. The actor predicts 117 the actions _a_ based on the graph and its node features _F_ . The node(superpixel) features are computed 118 by pooling together the corrresponding pixel features based on the raw image data.

3

- 119 Here, we make use of two different setups: Method 1, where the per-pixel features are computed 120 based on the image data with the feature extractor being part of the agent _A_ and Method 2 where the 121 feature extractor is part of the environment _E_ . The feature extractor is trained end-to-end in Method 122 1, whereas it is fixed and thus needs to be pre-trained in Method 2. We use a U-Net [40] as feature 123 extractor and can use hand-crafted features in addition to the learned features. More details about 124 the pre- training can be found in the Appendix. The agent - environment interaction for Method 1 is 125 depicted in Figure 1. For Method 2 we refer to the Appendix.

- 126 Importantly, this setup enables us to use both a non-differentiable instance segmentation step and 127 reward function, by encapsulation of the “pixels to instances” step in the environment and learning a 128 policy based on the rewards with a stateless actor critic.

- 129 **3.1 Stateless Reinforcement Learning Setup**

- 130 Unlike most RL settings [41], our approach does not require an explicitly time dependent state: the 131 actions returned by the agent correspond to the real-valued edge weights in [0 _,_ 1], which are used to 132 compute the graph partitioning. Any state can be reached by a single step from the initial state and 133 there exists no time dependency in the state transition. Unlike [9], we predict all edge values at once 134 which allows us to avoid the iterative strategy of [8] and deliver and evaluate a complete segmentation 135 in every step. We implement a stateless actor critic formulation with episodes of length 1.

- 136 To the best of our knowledge, stateless RL was introduced in [7] to study the connection between 137 generative adversarial networks and actor critics and our method is one of the first practical applica138 tions of this concept. Here, the agent consists of an actor, which predicts the actions _a_ and a critic, 139 which predicts the action value _Q_ (expected future discounted reward) given the actions. The stateless 140 approach simplifies the action value function: the action value has to estimate the reward for a single 141 step instead of estimating the expected sum of discounted future rewards for many steps. We have 142 explored a multi-step setup as well, but found that it yields inferior results for our application; details 143 can be found in the Appendix. As described in detail in 3.2, we compute localized sub-graph rewards 144 instead of relying on a single global reward.

145 The actor corresponds to a single GNN, which predicts the mean and variance of a normal distribution 146 for each edge. The actions _a_ are determined by sampling from this distribution and applying a 147 sigmoid to the result to obtain continuous edge weights in the value range [0 _,_ 1]. The GNN takes the 148 state _s_ = ( _G, F_ ) as input arguments and its graph convolution for the _i[th]_ node is defined as in [39]:

## 167 **3.2 Localized Supervision Signals**

168 The RL paradigm is to provide a global reward for a given state transition [41]. However, we find 169 that for our application it is possible and desirable to instead provide several more localized rewards 170 per state transition: Given a large action space with a policy represented by a complex multivariate 171 probability distribution, it is beneficial to learn from rewards for the specific actions rather than from 172 a scalar global reward for the union of all actions. Of course then requirement arises that the union of 173 local rewards must resemble to the global reward. E.g. the optimal policy is the same for local as for 174 the global reward.

175 Our actor critic setup (Section 3.1) expects rewards per sub-graph. A good set of sub-graphs should 176 fulfill the following requirements: each sub-graph should be connected so that the information 177 presented to the MLP computing the action value for this sub-graph is correlated. The size of 178 the sub-graphs, given by the number of edges, should be a parameter and all sub-graphs should 179 be extracted with exactly that size to serve as valid input for one of the MLPs. The union of all 180 sub-graphs should cover the complete graph so that each edge contributes to at least one action 181 value _Qsg_ . The sub-graphs should overlap to provide a smooth sum of action values. We introduce 182 Algorithm 1 to extract such a set of sub-graphs (see Appendix). Figure 2 shows the sub-graphs for a 183 small example graph.

184 While some of the rewards used in our experiments can be directly defined for the sub-graphs, most 185 are instead defined per object (see Appendix for details on reward design). We use the following 186 general procedure to map object-level rewards to sub-graphs: first assign to each superpixel the 187 reward of its corresponding object, then determine the reward per edge as the maximum value of its 188 two incident superpixels’ rewards and average the edge rewards to obtain the reward per sub-graph. 189 Here, we use the maximum because high object scores indicate that all actions contributing to the 190 respective object should get a high reward. However, for low object scores it is not possible to localize 191 the specific action responsible for the low score. Hence, by taking the maximum we assign the 192 higher score to edges whose incident superpixels belong to different objects, because they probably 193 correspond to a correct split. Note that the uncertainty in the assignment of low rewards can lead to 194 a noisy reward signal, but the averaging of the edge rewards over the sub-graphs and the overlaps

5

- 195 between the sub-graphs smooth and partially denoise the rewards. We have also explored a different 196 actor critic setup that can use object level rewards directly, eliminating the need for the sub-graph 197 extraction and mapping. However, this approach yields inferior results, see the Appendix for details.

## 198 **4 Experiments**

- 199 The agent of our setup acts on the superpixel graph and thus depends on the features assigned to the 200 nodes of the graph. We introduced two variants of our algorithm: in the base variant (Method 1) 201 we start from random features and make them part of the agent, allowing them to change through 202 back-propagation (Fig. 1). In contrast, Method 2 acts on predefined features which are provided 203 as part of the environment and are computed before training, e.g. through unsupervised clustering. 204 A very accurate clustering in the features produces an easy problem for the agent to solve where 205 even a global reward for all actions might be sufficient. However, in a real-world setting with no 206 supervision, the noisier the features become the more local the reward has to be. We evaluate Method 207 2 on synthetic data where self-supervised pretraining can deliver noisy, but meaningful node features. 208 Our full setup with Method 1 is evaluated on a dataset from a light microscopy experiment, where 209 highly regular object shapes are to be expected, but no good feature pre-training is possible.

- 210 To transform the edge weight predictions of the agent into an instance segmentation we use the 211 Multicut [42] algorithm. Here, other options are also possible such as hierarchical clustering used in 212 [9], but we choose the Multicut for its global optimality property. Hyperparameters of the pipeline 213 were found by cross-validation (see Appendix).

## 214 **4.1 Synthetic dataset: circles on structured ground**

- 215 To evaluate the feasibility of our approach, we create a synthetic dataset with prominent structured 216 background. Our aim is to segment irregular disks on such background using only rule-based 217 supervision. We generate the superpixels by the mutex watershed algorithm [25] which we run on 218 the Gaussian gradient image. The node features of the superpixel graph were computed through 219 self-supervised pretraining with contrastive loss as described in Appendix and fixed as part of the 220 environment.

- 221 As we aim to segment disks, we compute the circularity of the segmented objects for the rewards 222 using the Circle Hough Transform [43]. This object-level reward is combined with the global rough 223 estimate of the number of objects in the image to create the reward surface depicted in Fig. 3. The 224 reward for the number of objects provides useful gradient during early training stages: for example, 225 when too few potential objects are found in the prediction, a low reward can be given to what is 226 thought to be the background object. On the other hand, if too many potential objects are found, a 227 low reward can be given to all the foreground objects with a low CHT value.

228 In more detail, the object rewards _rfg_ are composed as follows. We define a threshold _γ_ on the CHT 229 value ( _γ_ = 0 _._ 8 in the reward surface shown in Fig. 3). ## 245 **4.2 Real dataset: light microscopy imaging**

- 246 Biomedical applications often require segmentation of objects of known morphology which are 247 positioned in regular patterns, while extensive prior knowledge is available on variability of both 248 under normal experimental conditions [44]. Such data presents the best use case for our algorithm as 249 the reward function can leverage the known characteristics of individual object shape and texture and 250 the overall similarity of the objects.

251 The dataset used for this experiment contains 317 2D images extracted from a video of a developing 252 fruitfly embryo acquired with a light-sheet microscope [45] (Fig. 5). The image shows boundaries 253 (plasma membranes) of the embryo cells. Across the dataset, 10 images were fully segmented by an 254 expert, we use those for validation.

255 Fruitfly embryo is a well-studied system for which we can exploit the prior knowledge on the expected 256 cell shape and the radial pattern of cells. Furthermore, as the analysis of cell shape dynamics is 257 a paramount part of many biological experiments, multiple pre-trained networks are available for 258 the cell segmentation task [18, 19, 46, 47]. Due to the differences in sample preparation and image 259 acquisition settings, none of these would work out-of-the-box for our data. However, the CNNs in 260 [47] which are trained to predict boundaries in confocal microscope images of plant tissue, can serve 261 as a strong edge detector to create superpixels in our images. The superpixels are obtained using the 262 seeded watershed algorithm on seeds at the local minima of the predicted edge map.

263 The rewards for this experiment are designed as follows: we set a high reward for merging the 264 superpixels which are certain to lie in the background (close to the image boundary or the image 265 center). For the background edges near the foreground area we modulate the reward by the circularity 266 of the overall foreground contour. Finally, for the edges which are likely to be in the foreground 267 we compute object-level rewards by fitting a rotated bounding box to each object and comparing its 268 side lengths as well as its orientation to predefined template values. We do not perform semantic 269 segmentation to define precise foreground/background boundaries, but instead use a soft weighting 270 scheme with Gaussian weights to combine object and background rewards based on on the prior

309 We compare to the following baseline approaches: _edge + mc_ , which solves the Multicut graph 310 partitioning based on edge weights derived from boundary predictions used for superpixel creation, 311 _contrastive_ , which predicts a pixel-wise embedding space that is clustered into instances using 312 k-means and for which the embeddings are trained using the discriminative loss function of [28] on 313 the ovules dataset from [47] and _contrastive + edge_ , which is similar to _contrastive_ , but receives the 314 [47] boundary predictions as additional input channel.

## 315 **5 Discussion**

- 316 We introduced an end-to-end instance segmentation algorithm which can exploit non-differentiable 317 loss functions and high-level prior information. Our RL approach is based on stateless actor-critic 318 and predicts the full segmentation at every step, allowing us to assign rewards to all objects and 319 reach stable convergence. The segmentation problem is formulated as graph partitioning; we design 320 a reward decomposition algorithm which maps object- and image-level rewards to sub-graphs for 321 localized supervision.

- 322 We performed proof-of-concept experiments to demonstrate the feasibility of our approach on 323 synthetic and real data and showed in particular that our setup can segment microscopy images 324 with no direct supervision other than high-level reasoning. In the future, we plan to explore other 325 problems and reward functions as well as a semi-supervised setup (briefly introduced in Appendix) 326 where we think our approach can be very beneficial. Furthermore, even in case of full supervision 327 with ample groundtruth, our RL-based formulation enables end-to-end instance segmentation with 328 direct object-level reasoning, which will allow for post-processing-aware training of the CNN which 329 predicts object boundaries or embeddings.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel and conceptually interesting approach to instance segmentation by leveraging stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning to incorporate non-differentiable high-level priors. The formulation of segmentation as graph partitioning with reward shaping based on object-level rules is well-motivated, particularly for biomedical applications where annotation is costly. The method demonstrates promising results on both synthetic and real microscopy data, and the ablation in Table 1 provides some evidence of the contribution of learned features and reward design.

Overview issues:

- Unclear mapping from edge weights to segmentation actions undermines training stability: The paper states that the actor predicts continuous edge weights in [0,1], which are then used by Multicut to produce a segmentation. However, it does not specify how these weights translate into merge/split decisions—specifically, whether low or high weights encourage merging. This ambiguity directly affects the reward signal: if the agent is rewarded for producing circular objects but the sign of the edge weight gradient is undefined, the policy cannot reliably learn. The visualization in Figure 4 uses green/red for merge/split, but the method does not define how sampling from the normal distribution (Eq 1) and sigmoid output maps to these discrete actions. Without a clear, differentiable (or at least consistent) thresholding or rounding rule, the environment's response to actions becomes stochastic and poorly aligned with policy updates, leading to high variance in gradients and unstable training.

- Reward decomposition method introduces bias and misattribution: The reward mapping from object-level scores to sub-graphs (Section 3.2) uses the maximum of incident superpixel rewards per edge, then averages over sub-graphs. This approach risks assigning high rewards to edges that split correct objects, as long as one incident superpixel belongs to a high-scoring object. The justification that 'high object scores should reward all contributing actions' is flawed—merging errors should not be rewarded even if they involve parts of well-formed objects. This design may reinforce incorrect edge predictions, especially at object boundaries. Moreover, the lack of a formal analysis or ablation of this reward assignment scheme leaves open the question of whether performance stems from effective learning or from accidental alignment of reward leakage.

- Evaluation lacks proper unsupervised baselines and overstates 'no supervision' claim: The paper claims 'excellent performance without any direct supervision', but the method relies heavily on pre-trained boundary detectors [47] for superpixel generation and hand-crafted geometric features (polar coordinates, mass). These constitute strong indirect supervision. The comparison to 'edge + mc [47]' uses the same boundary predictions, yet the proposed method is presented as unsupervised. Furthermore, baselines like contrastive embedding [28] are trained on a different dataset (ovules), making the comparison unfair. A true ablation would include a version using random or uniform features, or no pre-trained edge maps. The current evaluation overstates the autonomy of the method and underestimates the role of engineered inputs.

- Training objective conflicts with global segmentation quality: The use of localized sub-graph rewards (Section 3.2) risks optimizing for local consistency at the expense of global coherence. While the authors argue that overlapping sub-graphs smooth the signal, there is no mechanism to ensure that local rewards align with global metrics like Symmetric Best Dice or Variation of Information. This is evident in Table 1: the best-performing method still lags significantly behind the 'sp gt' upper bound, suggesting that superpixel merging decisions are suboptimal at scale. The reward in Eq 6 and Eq 8 depends on global object count, but this signal is diluted through sub-graph averaging. Without a hierarchical or iterative refinement strategy, the model may converge to locally optimal but globally fragmented segmentations.

- Insufficient justification for stateless setup and lack of convergence analysis: The paper claims the stateless actor-critic setup is advantageous, but provides no empirical or theoretical justification for why a single-step RL formulation outperforms multi-step or sequential approaches like [8]. The Appendix mentions inferior results for multi-step setups but gives no details. Moreover, the training curve in the Appendix (not shown in paper) is critical—without evidence of stable convergence across seeds and datasets, the method's reliability is questionable. The high variance typical of REINFORCE-style updates is not addressed, and no variance reduction techniques (e.g., baselines, advantage normalization) are mentioned, raising concerns about reproducibility.

- Overreliance on domain-specific priors limits generalizability: The reward functions in both experiments are deeply tailored to specific morphologies: circularity for synthetic disks and radial symmetry for fruitfly embryos. The method would likely fail on irregular or heterogeneous objects (e.g., neurons, tumors) where such priors do not hold. The paper does not explore how sensitive performance is to prior mismatch—e.g., what happens if the expected cell count is incorrect? This limits the method's applicability beyond highly controlled settings. A more robust framework would include adaptive prior estimation or uncertainty modeling, rather than hard-coded templates.

- No ablation of reward decomposition strategy: The paper introduces a reward decomposition method that maps object-level rewards to sub-graphs via max-pooling over incident superpixels and averaging. However, it provides no ablation comparing this strategy to alternatives, such as mean-pooling, attention-based weighting, or direct object-level reward backpropagation through a different GNN architecture. Readers will wonder whether performance depends on the specific choice of max-pooling or if it introduces harmful reward leakage, as high-scoring objects may inflate rewards for incorrect merges. To validate the design, the authors should compute and compare segmentation accuracy using at least one alternative mapping—e.g., assign edge rewards by averaging incident object scores instead of taking the maximum—and report the impact on Symmetric Best Dice in Table 1. Without this, the contribution of the decomposition strategy remains speculative.

- Missing comparison to sequential segmentation baselines: The paper claims advantages from predicting all edge weights in a single step, but it does not formally compare against iterative or sequential agglomeration methods like *[9]*, which also operate on superpixel graphs and could incorporate similar priors via reward shaping. A natural benchmark would be a stepwise merge policy trained with the same critic architecture but on a sequence of local decisions. The omission leaves open whether the stateless setup is genuinely better or merely different. The authors should implement a simplified version of *[9]* using their features and reward function, train it under comparable conditions, and report its final VI and SBD scores. This would ground the claimed efficiency and stability benefits in empirical evidence rather than design assertion.

- No test of prior robustness under mismatch: The method relies on strong domain-specific priors—e.g., expected object count and shape templates—but the paper does not evaluate how performance degrades when these priors are inaccurate. For real-world applicability, it must be shown that the system fails gracefully. For instance, what happens if the assumed number of cells in the fruitfly embryo is off by 20%? The authors should rerun the microscopy experiment with perturbed template values—e.g., incorrect expected orientation or cell count—and plot the drop in SBD score. This would demonstrate whether the framework is brittle or can adapt through reward balancing. Without such a test, the practical utility of the approach in variable biological conditions remains unproven.

- Absence of computational cost and scalability discussion: The paper reports training for four days on a high-end GPU but gives no details on inference speed, memory use, or scaling with image size. Given that graph partitioning and GNN inference on large superpixel graphs can be expensive, readers will question whether the method is feasible beyond small 2D slices. The authors should report runtime per image during inference, memory footprint, and how sub-graph size affects training stability. Specifically, they should time the Multicut solver and GNN forward pass on images of increasing resolution (e.g., 256×256, 512×512) and state whether batching is possible. This information is essential for judging practical adoption in biomedical pipelines.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the mapping from predicted edge weights to merge/split actions and ensure consistency in the reward signal.

- Re-evaluate the reward decomposition strategy with ablations on alternative aggregation methods (e.g., mean, min) and analyze reward attribution.

- Include a true unsupervised baseline (e.g., random features, no pre-trained edges) and reframe claims about supervision level.

- Provide convergence curves, variance across seeds, and comparison to multi-step RL variants to justify the stateless design.

- Assess robustness to prior misspecification and discuss limitations in generalizing to irregular object shapes.

comments.title: Ambiguous edge weight semantics break policy learning
comments.quote: We formulate the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position or size.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify whether high or low predicted edge weights encourage merging or splitting in the Multicut solver. In standard formulations, low weights typically indicate boundary evidence and promote cuts, while high weights favor merging. Without this mapping, the sign of the reward gradient with respect to edge predictions is undefined, making policy updates ill-posed. For instance, if the agent is rewarded for circularity but does not know whether to increase or decrease edge weights to merge regions, learning cannot proceed reliably. The method must explicitly define this relationship—e.g., 'low weights indicate strong boundary evidence and trigger splits'—to ensure coherent credit assignment.

comments.title: Max-pooling reward assignment promotes incorrect merges
comments.quote: Here, we use the maximum because high object scores indicate that all actions contributing to the respective object should get a high reward. However, for low object scores it is not possible to localize the specific action responsible for the low score. Hence, by taking the maximum we assign the higher score to edges whose incident superpixels belong to different objects, because they probably correspond to a correct split.
comments.explanation: The justification for max-pooling edge rewards is logically flawed. Consider an edge between superpixels from different objects: one with high reward (well-formed) and one with low. The max operation assigns a high reward to this edge. But if high rewards encourage merging (via high edge weights), this rule reinforces merging across object boundaries—exactly the wrong behavior. A correct split should be encouraged by a low reward or penalty, not a high one. The paper conflates high reward with desirable action without specifying whether the actor learns to include or exclude edges. This design risks reinforcing false positives at object boundaries and undermines segmentation fidelity.

comments.title: Critic loss uses undefined global reward symbol
comments.quote:
The loss for the critic is given by:

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\text {c r i t i c}} = \frac {1}{| S G |} \sum_ {s g \in G} \frac {1}{2} \left(Q _ {s g} ^ {\delta} (s, a) - r\right) ^ {2} \tag {4}
$$
comments.explanation: The symbol $r$ in Equation (4) is undefined. Given the context of sub-graph rewards, it should represent a localized reward $r_{sg}$ for each sub-graph $sg$. However, the notation $r$ without subscript suggests a single global scalar, which contradicts the summation over sub-graphs. Moreover, the sum is written as $\sum_{sg \in G}$, but $G$ denotes the full graph while $SG$ is the set of sub-graphs—this is a notational inconsistency. The critic loss must compare $Q_{sg}^\delta(s,a)$ to a corresponding target $r_{sg}$. Without subscripting $r$ and correcting the sum to $\sum_{sg \in SG}$, the equation is ambiguous and mathematically ill-typed.

comments.title: Actor loss gradient misaligned with policy gradient theory
comments.quote:

$\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_{actor}=\nabla_{\theta}\frac{1}{|SG|}\sum_{sg\in G}\left[\alpha\sum_{\hat{a}\in sg}log(\pi^{\theta}(\hat{a}|s))-Q_{sg}(s,a)\right]$
comments.explanation:
The actor loss gradient in Equation (2) does not follow standard policy gradient theory. In entropy-regularized actor-critic methods, the update should be proportional to $
abla_	heta \log \pi^	heta(a|s) \cdot A(s,a)$, where $A$ is an advantage estimate. Here, the gradient is applied to a sum where $Q_{sg}(s,a)$ is subtracted from $\log \pi^	heta$, and the entire expression is differentiated with respect to $	heta$. Since $Q_{sg}$ is independent of $	heta$, this reduces to $
abla_	heta \sum \log \pi^	heta$, which only encourages increasing log-probability through the entropy term. The correct form should involve multiplication, not subtraction. Rewrite as $

abla_{	heta} \log \pi^	heta(a_{sg}|s) \cdot (Q_{sg}(s,a) - b_{sg})$ to align with policy gradient principles.

comments.title: Incorrect background reward formulation in Eq 9
comments.quote:
For each edge, we define the distance  $h$  between the edge and the center of the image as the average distance of the incident objects' center of mass and the center  $c$  of the image.  $j$  is the approximate radius of the circle that lies within the foreground and  $m$  is the maximal distance between  $c$  and the image boarder. Let further  $\mathcal{K}(\cdot)$  be the Gaussian kernel function. Then  $r_{edge}$  yields

$$
r _ {b g} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \mathcal {K} \left(\frac {\left| h - c \right|}{\gamma}\right) (1 - a), & \text {if} h \leq j \\ \mathcal {K} \left(\frac {\left| m - h \right|}{\eta}\right) (1 - a), & \text {otw} \end{array} \right. \tag {9}
$$
comments.explanation: The expression $|h - c|$ in Eq (9) is ill-defined: $h$ is a scalar distance while $c$ is a vector (image center), creating a type mismatch. The correct form should use radial distance $r = \|x - c\|$ for each superpixel. Additionally, $m$ is defined as the maximal distance from $c$ to the border—i.e., the image radius $R$—so $|m - h|$ should be $|R - h|$. But the first case uses $|h - c|$, which is invalid. The entire formulation should be rewritten using consistent radial coordinates. For example: define $r$ as the radial distance from center, and set $r_{bg} = \mathcal{K}(|r|/\gamma)(1-a)$ if $r \leq j$, else $\mathcal{K}(|R - r|/\eta)(1-a)$. The current version misuses vector $c$ in a scalar context.

comments.title: Global reward definition contradicts stated bounds
comments.quote: The rewards are always in $[0,1]$ here this is split up into $[0,0.5]$ for the local reward as well as for the global reward.
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by Equation (6), where $r_{global} = 0.6$ when $n < k$, exceeding the stated 0.5 upper bound. Similarly, Equation (8) sets $r_{bg} = 1$ otherwise, which is within $[0,1]$ but violates the partitioning claim. The authors cannot assert that both components are limited to $[0,0.5]$ while using values above that. This undermines the interpretability of the reward design and suggests a lack of consistency in normalization. The sentence should be removed or corrected to reflect actual ranges: $r_{local} \in [0, 0.4)$, $r_{global} \in (0, 0.6]$, and $r_{bg} \in (0,1]$.

comments.title: Ambiguous background object selection invalidates reward logic
comments.quote: For the largest predicted object we strongly suspect the background object. For this object background rewards $r_{bg}$ are calculated by
comments.explanation: The assumption that the largest predicted object is the background is not always valid in the 'circles on structured ground' setup. The structured background may fragment, and a large foreground disk could be the largest connected component. Without additional criteria (e.g., low circularity, texture), labeling the largest object as background risks assigning $r_{bg}$ to a well-formed foreground object, penalizing correct segmentation. This flaw invalidates the reward logic in non-trivial cases. The method should require the largest object to also exhibit background-like features (e.g., low CHT value) before applying $r_{bg}$, or justify why size alone suffices in this dataset.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'otw' in piecewise functions
comments.quote:
r_{local}=\begin{cases}\sigma\left((\frac{c-\gamma}{1-\gamma}-0.5)6\right)0.4,&\text{if }c\geq\gamma\\
0,&\text{otw}\end{cases} \] (5)
\[ r_{global}=\begin{cases}r_{exp}\left(\frac{k}{n}\right),&\text{if }n\geq k\\
0.6,&\text{otw}\end{cases} \] (6)
comments.explanation: The abbreviation 'otw' is used inconsistently: in Eq (5), it covers $c < \gamma$, assigning $r_{local} = 0$; in Eq (6), it covers $n < k$, assigning $r_{global} = 0.6$; in Eq (8), 'otw' covers $n > k$, assigning $r_{bg} = 1$. This creates a logical asymmetry without clear motivation. The reward behavior is not symmetric, and the functional forms (exponential vs. constant) are not aligned. Moreover, 'otw' should be replaced with standard notation like 'else'. The lack of symmetry and inconsistent conditions needs justification—otherwise, the reward design appears arbitrary.

comments.title: Undeclared dependency on pre-trained boundary detector
comments.quote: The superpixels are obtained using the seeded watershed algorithm on seeds at the local minima of the predicted edge map.
comments.explanation: The method relies on a pre-trained boundary detector [47] to generate superpixels, which constitutes indirect supervision. This contradicts the paper's repeated claim of 'no direct supervision'. The boundary detector alone encodes strong spatial priors learned from annotated data, significantly shaping the solution space. The paper must acknowledge this dependency. Add: 'The edge map is generated by a pre-trained boundary detector [47], which provides indirect supervision and constrains the space of achievable segmentations.' Otherwise, the claim of autonomy from labeled data is misleading.

comments.title: Misleading claim of end-to-end differentiability
comments.quote: Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction to differentiable losses and - regardless of the loss - to make the whole pipeline differentiable end-to-end even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into individual instances.
comments.explanation: The claim that the pipeline becomes 'differentiable end-to-end' is incorrect. Differentiability requires gradients to flow through all operations, but the Multicut solver and reward computation are discrete and non-differentiable. The actor-critic uses policy gradients estimated via Monte Carlo sampling, not backpropagation through the environment. RL circumvents the need for differentiability—it does not make the pipeline differentiable. This distinction is crucial. Rewrite as: 'to circumvent the restriction to differentiable losses by enabling policy updates in the presence of non-differentiable steps.' The current phrasing misrepresents the learning mechanism.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of stable convergence
comments.quote: Our RL approach is based on stateless actor-critic and predicts the full segmentation at every step, allowing us to assign rewards to all objects and reach stable convergence.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'stable convergence' lacks empirical support. REINFORCE-style algorithms typically suffer from high variance, especially in stateless setups without temporal credit assignment. The paper mentions training curves in the Appendix but does not include them or report metrics across seeds. Without convergence plots or statistical evaluation of policy performance over time, readers cannot verify stability. The claim should be tempered to 'achieve convergence in our experiments' unless supported by evidence.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a stateless actor-critic RL framework for instance segmentation that can incorporate non-differentiable high-level priors. While the approach is novel and addresses an important limitation in weakly supervised segmentation, there are significant concerns about the validation methodology, generalization claims, and the practical feasibility of the proposed solution.

The paper makes a meaningful contribution by demonstrating how stateless actor-critic RL can enable non-differentiable priors in instance segmentation, which is a genuinely innovative approach. The synthetic experiments provide proof-of-concept, and the microscopy application shows potential real-world utility. However, the evaluation is limited to a single real dataset with minimal validation data, and the computational efficiency claims are not substantiated.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient validation on real microscopy data: The paper evaluates on only 10 annotated images from a single microscopy dataset (Section 4.2), which is inadequate to demonstrate robustness. For a method claiming generalization to microscopy applications, this tiny validation set cannot establish statistical significance or account for biological variability. The paper needs additional validation on at least 2-3 diverse microscopy datasets with more annotated images to support the generalization claims. Without this, it's unclear whether the observed performance is specific to this particular fruitfly embryo dataset or generalizes to other microscopy modalities.

- Unjustified assumption about stateless approach capturing spatial dependencies: The stateless formulation (Section 3.1) assumes that predicting all edge weights simultaneously can capture complex spatial dependencies in segmentation. However, instance segmentation inherently involves sequential decisions where early merges affect later possibilities. The paper provides no theoretical or empirical justification for why the stateless approach is sufficient. A comparison with a recurrent/stateful baseline is needed to validate this design choice. The claim that multi-step setups yield inferior results (mentioned briefly in Appendix) should be moved to the main paper with detailed analysis.

- Questionable synthetic-to-real domain transfer: The synthetic "circles on structured ground" dataset (Section 4.1) appears too simplistic to represent real microscopy challenges. The circles are uniform objects on structured backgrounds, while real microscopy involves variable shapes, textures, and noise patterns. The paper needs to demonstrate why performance on this synthetic task predicts real-world performance. Including a more complex synthetic dataset that better approximates microscopy variability, or providing analysis of the domain gap, would strengthen the validation.

- Missing computational efficiency analysis: Despite claiming computational efficiency advantages, the paper provides no timing analysis or scalability comparison. Training for 4 days on a single GPU (Section 4.2) suggests the method may be computationally expensive. The paper should report training and inference times, memory requirements, and compare with baselines. For practical microscopy applications where throughput matters, this analysis is essential to assess feasibility.

- Inadequate ablation of high-level priors: The paper claims that high-level priors drive performance but provides limited ablation studies. While Table 1 shows some feature ablations, there's no systematic evaluation of different prior components (shape, position, size) or their relative importance. The paper needs to analyze how sensitive performance is to each prior type and to the prior strength parameters (γ, η, δ in Equations 9-10). This would demonstrate whether the approach truly benefits from high-level reasoning or is mostly learning from low-level features.

- Limited comparison with state-of-the-art instance segmentation methods: The baselines in Table 1 are primarily older methods or simple variants. Missing are comparisons with modern instance segmentation approaches like Mask R-CNN, DETR, or recent transformer-based methods adapted for microscopy. While these methods require full supervision, comparing their performance with minimal supervision would contextualize the trade-offs. The paper should include at least one state-of-the-art supervised method to establish the performance gap being traded for reduced supervision.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous priors: The paper needs to show that its high-level priors actually provide meaningful guidance beyond what low-level features can achieve. This requires an example where the priors are violated by a specific DGP that fails the condition being tested. For instance, the authors should create a synthetic dataset where objects violate the circularity or size priors (e.g., elliptical cells or irregular nuclei) and demonstrate that the method fails or produces degraded segmentations. Without such a failure case, it's unclear whether the priors are truly driving performance or if the method is mostly learning from low-level appearance.

- No worked parametric example: The paper lacks a concrete, fully-specified model where readers can see the complete pipeline in action. The authors should compute their main quantities (edge weights, rewards, Q-values) for a simple parametric case - perhaps a 3x3 superpixel graph with known object shapes. This would help readers understand how the stateless actor-critic actually works in practice. For example, show step-by-step how the Circle Hough Transform reward is computed for a specific configuration of superpixels, how this maps to subgraph rewards, and how the actor's policy updates.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper claims practical relevance but provides insufficient guidance for applying the method to new microscopy domains. Missing are concrete recommendations for: choosing appropriate priors for different cell types, setting the normalization constants (γ, η, δ) in Equations 9-10, determining optimal subgraph sizes for different image resolutions, and troubleshooting when priors conflict. The authors should include a 'practitioner's guide' section with specific heuristics derived from their experiments, making the method reproducible beyond their specific datasets.

- Missing verification of stateless advantage: The paper claims stateless RL is advantageous but never formally demonstrates this. The authors should verify that their approach recovers the results of a stateful baseline as a special case, or provide theoretical justification for why stateless is sufficient. Specifically, they should compare against a recurrent actor-critic on the same tasks and quantify the trade-offs in performance vs. computational efficiency. The brief mention in Appendix that multi-step performs worse needs to be expanded with detailed analysis of why stateless works better in this context.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Expand validation to include multiple microscopy datasets with at least 50 annotated images total

- Provide theoretical justification and empirical comparison for the stateless versus stateful design choice

- Add computational efficiency analysis including training/inference times and memory usage

- Conduct systematic ablation studies of the high-level priors and their parameters

- Include comparison with at least one modern supervised instance segmentation method

comments.title: Incorrect claim about making pipeline differentiable
comments.quote: Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction to differentiable losses and - regardless of the loss - to make the whole pipeline differentiable end-to-end even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into individual instances.
comments.explanation: This claim is mathematically inaccurate. Reinforcement learning with policy gradients enables optimization through non-differentiable components by treating them as part of the environment, but it does not make these operations differentiable. The graph partitioning step remains non-differentiable; what changes is that the learning algorithm does not require gradients through this step. Rewrite as: "Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction to differentiable losses and enable end-to-end learning even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into individual instances, by treating these steps as part of the environment and using policy gradient methods that do not require differentiability through the entire pipeline."
